title: Chrysanthemum mutellina
what_description: Chrysanthemum mutellina is a perennial plant belonging to the family Asteraceae. It is characterized by its yellow flowers with 3-5 ray florets, which are 1-2 mm long, and 10-20 yellow disc florets, which are 0.5-1 mm long. The plant's leaves are lance-shaped, 2-5 cm long, and 3-5 mm wide, with a pointed tip and a serrated margin. Chrysanthemum mutellina grows up to 1 m tall and 0.5 m wide, with a hairy stem and leaf margins. The plant is native to the Balkan Peninsula and is found in dry, rocky areas.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Chrysanthemum mutellina are inflammation, fever, diarrhea and rheumatism, which have been effectively treated by its antiphlogistic, antipyretic, anti-diarrheal and anti-arthritic properties. Additionally, its expectorant properties help alleviate cough and asthma symptoms, while its anxiolytic and adaptogenic properties relieve anxiety and digestive issues. Furthermore, Chrysanthemum mutellina has been used to treat skin conditions, gastroenteritis, sore throat and allergies due to its anti-inflammatory, anti-bacterial and anti-fungal properties. Its anti-irritant properties also soothe eczema, insect bites, skin irritations and dyspepsia, while its anti-inflammatory and anti-arthritic properties help alleviate bronchitis and arthritis. Moreover, its adaptogenic properties also help regulate menstrual issues and viral infections.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Chrysanthemum mutellina are attributed to its antioxidant activity, which is primarily due to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids, and is responsible for protecting the body from oxidative stress and damage. This plant exhibits significant antimicrobial properties, particularly against gram-positive bacteria, which is attributed to the bioactive compounds saponins and triterpenoids. Its anti-inflammatory properties are also noteworthy, as they are mediated by flavonoids, which can inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines. Moreover, Chrysanthemum mutellina has been found to possess analgesic and antipyretic properties, which are attributed to the presence of volatile oils, such as borneol and camphor, that can help to reduce pain and fever. The combination of these bioactive compounds is thought to contribute to the plant's therapeutic potential in treating various health conditions.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Chrysanthemum mutellina are flavonoids, triterpenoids, quercetin, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Flavonoids, particularly quercetin, have been shown to exhibit antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities, contributing to the plant's potential in treating various conditions. Triterpenoids, such as ursolic acid and oleanolic acid, possess anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and hepatoprotective properties. Phenolic acids, including ferulic acid and sinapic acid, have been demonstrated to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Saponins, like chrysanthemin, have been reported to have anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and immunomodulatory activities, making Chrysanthemum mutellina a valuable source of bioactive compounds.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Chrysanthemum mutellina are chrysanthemum tea, infusion, tincture, decoction and salve. Chrysanthemum tea is prepared by steeping the flowers in hot water to make a floral tea used to treat fever and cough. An infusion is made by steeping the flowers in cold water to produce a calming and anti-inflammatory drink. A tincture is prepared by soaking the flowers in a solvent such as ethanol to produce a concentrated liquid extract used for treating fever and digestive issues. A decoction is made by boiling the flowers in water to produce a strong liquid extract used to treat colds and respiratory issues, while a salve is prepared by mixing the flowers with a carrier oil to produce a topical cream used for treating skin irritations and wounds.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Chrysanthemum mutellina improperly include inducing allergic reactions, triggering asthma attacks, developing skin irritation and allergic reactions due to its potent allergenic compounds, and experiencing diarrhea due to its potential impact on the digestive system, particularly when consumed in excess or without proper preparation. Chrysanthemum mutellina contains sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which can cause anaphylactic reactions and exacerbate respiratory conditions in sensitive individuals. Its allergenic properties can also lead to skin irritation, including redness, itching, and rashes, especially in people with pre-existing skin conditions. Furthermore, its potential toxicity can cause gastrointestinal upset, including diarrhea and stomach cramps, particularly when ingested in large quantities or with other medications. Improper use of Chrysanthemum mutellina can also lead to interactions with other herbs and medications, exacerbating its side effects and increasing the risk of adverse reactions.
uses: Chrysanthemum mutellina, also known as the carline thistle, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. Its roots, in particular, are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. In some cultures, it is used to treat conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin irritations. Additionally, it is said to have a calming effect on the nervous system and can be used as a natural sedative. However, more research is needed to confirm its medicinal efficacy.
taxonomy: Chrysanthemum mutellina is classified as a species within the genus Chrysanthemum, which is a part of the family Asteraceae. This classification places it among the daisy-like flowering plants. The genus Chrysanthemum is a group of flowering plants that are often cultivated for their ornamental value. Chrysanthemum mutellina is native to Eastern Europe and Western Asia, where it thrives in dry, rocky areas. The species is a perennial plant that produces yellow flowers in late summer.
benefits: Chrysanthemum mutellina, also known as Yellow Chrysanthemum or Hungarian Chrysanthemum, has been used in traditional medicine for various health benefits. Its flowers and leaves are rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, which may help reduce inflammation and improve cardiovascular health. The plant has also been used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and constipation, due to its anti-inflammatory properties. In addition, Chrysanthemum mutellina has been used to relieve fever and coughs, making it a popular remedy for respiratory issues. Furthermore, its antiseptic properties may help protect against infections and promote wound healing.
parts: Chrysanthemum mutellina, a flowering plant species, is used in traditional medicine, particularly in Eastern Europe and Asia. The aerial parts, including leaves and flowers, are typically used for medicinal purposes. These parts are believed to possess anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antispasmodic properties. The leaves and flowers are often used to treat various health issues, such as fever, digestive problems, and respiratory conditions. In some traditional practices, the plant's extracts are used to treat wounds and skin conditions.
intro: Chrysanthemum mutellina, also known as Yellow Chamomile, is a flowering plant species within the daisy family (Asteraceae). The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, coughs, and respiratory issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties. The bioactive constituents of Chrysanthemum mutellina include flavonoids, sesquiterpenes, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Chrysanthemum mutellina include teas, infusions, and extracts, often used to promote relaxation and alleviate symptoms of the common cold and flu.
